planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in full sun; Kuwini mango is a Southeast Asian relative of common mango producing smaller, intensely fragrant, oval fruits with an aroma so powerful and distinctive that it can be detected from meters away — the fruits are primarily used for juice and drinks rather than eaten out of hand due to the fibrous flesh.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in full sun; Kuwini mango is similar in requirements to common mango but more frost-sensitive. Mangifera odorata (Kuwini mango or fragrant mango) is a species in the Anacardiaceae family related to common mango (M. indica), native to the Malay Peninsula and the islands of Borneo and Sumatra, producing small to medium-sized, oval, green to yellowish fruits with extraordinarily intense mango-floral fragrance — the most powerfully aromatic of all Mangifera species. The fresh fruit has a strong turpentine-floral aroma detectable at a considerable distance, with yellow-orange flesh that is significantly more fibrous than commercial mangoes and with more resinous flavor notes. While less pleasant to eat out of hand than polished commercial mango varieties, Kuwini is prized in Malaysia and Indonesia for juice, juice blends, mango lassi-type drinks, ice cream, and traditional sambal sauces where the intense fragrance shines through processing. The skin of the fruit also yields a yellow dye. The tree is also notable as one of the parents in the natural hybrid origin of common mango's wild ancestors. In zone 11–12 tropical fruit collections, Kuwini is a valuable diversity addition for those wanting the full spectrum of Mangifera species. Very rare in cultivation outside Southeast Asia.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops — Mangifera odorata
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online
Malaysian Agricultural Research and Development Institute (MARDI)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in full sun; Wani or Binjai mango is a Borneo and Sumatra native Mangifera species producing large, pale-green to whitish-green, very heavy fruits with sweet-tart, somewhat turpentine-flavored white to pale-yellow flesh — one of the most important traditional fruit trees of Malaysian Borneo.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in full sun; requires consistent rainfall or irrigation during fruit development. Mangifera caesia (wani mango or binjai) is a large Mangifera species native to the humid lowland rainforests of Borneo, Sumatra, and the Malay Peninsula — one of the most important traditional fruit trees of Sarawak and Sabah (Malaysian Borneo), where it is planted around villages and sometimes bears enormous quantities of fruits. The fruits are remarkable: large (4–6 inches long), elongated-oblong to kidney-shaped, pale green to yellowish-white when ripe — larger on average than common mangoes — with white to pale-yellow flesh. The flavor is more distinctly turpentine-acidic than common mango, with a sweetness that varies considerably by seedling; selected clones can be mild and pleasant. The flesh is rather fibrous and the skin is not eaten. In Sarawak, wani fruits are sold fresh in markets and eaten as a fresh fruit, in rujak (spicy fruit salads), and processed into pickles. The bark and young leaves have traditional medicinal uses in Malaysian herbal medicine. Wani requires a genuinely tropical, hot, humid, low-frost-risk climate and is suitable only for zone 11–12 in the continental United States (extreme South Florida, Hawaii). A collectors' fruit tree for the most dedicated tropical fruit enthusiast.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops — Mangifera caesia
Sarawak Biodiversity Centre — Indigenous Fruit Trees
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online

common_name: Brush Cherry (Magenta Brush Cherry, Australian Cherry)
subcategory: tropical evergreen hedge and fruit shrub

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Brush cherry is an Australian native Syzygium grown in zone 9–12 primarily as an ornamental hedge and specimen tree, but producing abundant small, bright magenta-pink, edible fruits with a mild, slightly astringent, rose-water-like flavor.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; shape with hedge shears for formal use or allow to grow naturally as a specimen. Syzygium paniculatum (brush cherry or magenta brush cherry) is an Australian native tree or large shrub in the rose apple family (Myrtaceae) — closely related to rose apple (S. jambos) and other Syzygium fruit trees — that has become one of the most widely planted evergreen hedge, screen, and topiary plants in zone 9–12 landscapes in Florida, California, and the Gulf Coast. The plant produces glossy, lance-shaped, dark-green new growth that flushes copper-red to bronze-red as it emerges — making it attractive year-round — and responds well to shearing into formal hedge shapes. It produces abundant, 1/2-inch, bright magenta-pink fruits in summer that are edible but primarily astringent and mild, with a rose-water and mild apple flavor; they are eaten fresh in small quantities, made into jams, or used in drinks. The flowers are fluffy, cream-white, and produced in clusters; they attract bees and butterflies. As a hedge plant, brush cherry is outstanding: it maintains dense, dark-green foliage, responds well to clipping, and is salt-tolerant and drought-tolerant once established. More widely available in the nursery trade in Florida and California than most other Syzygium species.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Syzygium paniculatum
California Polytechnic State University — SelecTree Database

common_name: Buttercup Tree (Poro Poro, Rose Silk Cotton)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; Buttercup tree is a Central American tropical deciduous tree producing one of the most spectacular floral displays in the tropical world — large (3–4 inch), brilliant-yellow, poppy-like flowers with silky yellow petals appearing on bare, leafless branches in the dry season — similar in effect to yellow silk floss tree or yellow jacaranda.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; drought-tolerant once established; the tree naturally defoliates in a dry season to bloom. Cochlospermum vitifolium (buttercup tree or poro poro) is a Mexican and Central American tropical deciduous tree that creates one of the most dramatic dry-season flowering displays in the tropical world. The tree is deciduous in response to drought or cooler temperatures: as it drops its large, deeply-lobed, vine-like leaves (the species name 'vitifolium' means 'grape-leaved,' referring to the leaf shape), the bare branches produce large (3–4 inch across), brilliant golden-yellow flowers with five silky, crinkled petals and prominent yellow stamens — like enormous buttercups or golden poppies — covering the entire leafless tree in a blaze of yellow. The effect is similar to a yellow Tabebuia or yellow jacaranda — a complete crown of golden flowers on bare branches — spectacularly showy. The tree is fast-growing, drought-tolerant, and appropriate for tropical dry landscapes; it can actually flower more prolifically with a dry season. Fluffy, cotton-like seed fibers in the large seed capsules provide the name 'silk cotton.' Bark fibers have traditional uses in rope and textile making in Mexico. An exceptional and underused ornamental for zone 10–12 tropical gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Ornamental Trees

common_name: Silky Oak (Australian Silver Oak, Fern-Leaf Grevillea)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; silky oak is a spectacular Australian native tree with deeply-cut, fern-like, silver-green foliage and brilliant orange-yellow brush flowers — it grows rapidly to 60–80 feet and is widely used as a street tree in zone 8–11 throughout Florida, California, South Africa, and India.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; silky oak is drought-tolerant and fast-growing — site with ample space. Grevillea robusta (silky oak or Australian silver oak) is a large, fast-growing Australian native tree from the subtropical rainforests of Queensland and New South Wales — one of the most widely planted and recognizable Australian trees in subtropical and warm-temperate regions worldwide. The tree produces extraordinarily beautiful, deeply pinnate to bi-pinnate, fern-like leaves with a silver-silky underside (the 'silky' in the name) that create a delicate, lacy, architectural texture rare in a tree of this size. In spring, the tree is covered with striking, elongated clusters of golden-orange to rusty-orange 'brush' flowers (each a typical Proteaceae flower with a long, curling style) that attract bees, butterflies, and in Australia, honeyeaters. In the US, silky oak is used as a street tree and large specimen in zone 8–11 Florida and California, where it grows rapidly to 50–80 feet. In India, silky oak is one of the most commonly planted shade trees on tea plantations, providing canopy shade for tea bushes (and its wood is the traditional material for Indian furniture in colonial-era designs). It tolerates lean, acidic soils and drought once established — very low maintenance once past establishment. CAUTION: Contact dermatitis reported in some individuals from handling leaves or wood.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Grevillea
Royal Botanic Garden Sydney — Australian Native Trees
California Polytechnic State University — SelecTree Database
